steps.title: Identify the claim
steps.content:
The underlined claim states that male and female A. fasciatus share the same sponge microhabitat. To support this, the data must show both males and females present together in the same sponge — across multiple sponges, not just one.
  • Choice A — ‘Each of the three sponges was inhabited by at least one male A. fasciatus and at least one undetermined A. fasciatus.’: This mentions males and undetermined sharks, but says nothing about females. It cannot support a male-female cohabitation claim. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘Sponge 1 was inhabited by 17 male A. fasciatus.’: This gives only a male count from one sponge, with no information about females at all. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘Each of the three sponges was inhabited by at least one male A. fasciatus and at least one female A. fasciatus.’: This directly shows males and females sharing the same sponge across all three microhabitats studied, which is exactly what the claim requires. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘Sponge 1 was inhabited by 13 female A. fasciatus.’: This gives only a female count from one sponge, with no information about males. ✗

steps.title: Confirm the match
steps.content: Only the credited statement, ‘Each of the three sponges was inhabited by at least one male A. fasciatus and at least one female A. fasciatus,’ shows both sexes present in the same microhabitat across all three sponges, directly proving the cohabitation pattern described in the passage. That confirms C is correct.
